description: HLLQP - LIFE 03 QZ - Whole Life and Term-100 Insurance
examTips: Focus on whole life policy features (guaranteed premium, CSV, non-forfeiture options), T-100 characteristics, participating vs non-participating policies, dividend options, and premium payment structures.

questions.question:
Michael is 35 years old. He applied for $100,000 in whole life insurance. The insurer crunched the numbers and quoted him a premium of $162 per month. Michael will have to pay the premium every month, and it will never increase.
On a whole life policy, what is the primary purpose of the policy reserve?
options: A. To provide for the payment of future death claims.
B. To pay for the insurer's administrative expenses.
C. To provide a source of policy dividends.
D. To fund the cash surrender value of the policy.
optionFeedback: The reserve is not primarily intended to pay the insurer's administrative expenses.
Policy dividends may come from surplus on participating policies, but that is not the primary purpose of the reserve.
Cash surrender value is supported by the reserve, but the reserve's primary purpose is to ensure future death claims can be paid.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The reserve is not primarily intended to pay the insurer's administrative expenses; Policy dividends may come from surplus on participating policies, but that is not the primary purpose of the reserve.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: To provide for the payment of future death claims.

questions.question:
Caleb is 50 and did not even consider insurance until he had a son, named Turner, a few years ago. Caleb has lived a very exciting life as the lead singer in a mildly successful band. While Caleb makes a decent living, it is unlikely that he will ever strike it rich. His lifestyle has caused him to gain weight, which has contributed to high blood pressure and type 2 diabetes.
While he was still able to obtain life insurance, the policy was "rated", which resulted in a higher-than-usual premium. Caleb inquires if there is anything he can do today to ensure Turner can have some form of coverage in early adulthood regardless of his (Turner's) health.
Which of the following strategies would be appropriate?
options: A. Caleb cannot purchase life insurance in Turner's name because his son has not reached the age of majority.

B. Caleb can provide the insurer with evidence of insurability for Turner today. This will lock in Turner's right to purchase life insurance throughout his life without providing evidence of insurability.
C. Caleb could purchase a whole life insurance policy with himself as the life-insured and policyholder. When Turner is older, Caleb can change the life-insured to Turner.
D. Caleb could purchase a life insurance policy with Turner as the life-insured. When his son is older, Caleb can absolutely assign (transfer) the policy to him.
optionFeedback: A parent can purchase life insurance on a minor child; the age of majority is not a barrier.
Evidence of insurability for the insured today does not lock in future insurability for a different person (Turner).
The life-insured cannot simply be changed to a different person on an existing policy.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A parent can purchase life insurance on a minor child; the age of majority is not a barrier; Evidence of insurability for the insured today does not lock in future insurability for a different person (Turner).
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Caleb could purchase a life insurance policy with Turner as the life-insured. When his son is older, Caleb can absolutely assign (transfer) the policy to him.

questions.question:
Harvey has a $500,000 whole life policy with over $90,000 in cash value. The annual premium of $6,000 is significantly more than what a 10-year term policy would cost at his age. Harvey has just been told by his doctor that he has cancer, and his life expectancy is less than two years. He has had second and third opinions that confirm the prognosis. Harvey is confident that he will be dead within a few years. He would like to eliminate the premium entirely but also ensure that the beneficiaries receive exactly $500,000.
Which of the following options, if any, would allow for this?
options: A. There is no way to eliminate the premium without reducing the eventual death benefit.
B. The reduced paid-up insurance non-forfeiture option
C. The extended term insurance non-forfeiture option
D. The automatic premium loan (APL) feature
optionFeedback: The extended term insurance option does eliminate premiums while keeping the full face amount intact.
Reduced paid-up insurance eliminates premiums but reduces the death benefit to a lesser permanent amount.
The APL keeps the policy in force but creates a growing loan that reduces the net death benefit below $500,000.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The extended term insurance option does eliminate premiums while keeping the full face amount intact; Reduced paid-up insurance eliminates premiums but reduces the death benefit to a lesser permanent amount.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.

questions.question:
Grace purchased a participating whole life insurance policy over 20 years ago. Each time a policy dividend was paid, it was used to buy an additional piece of paid-up insurance. Her agent has advised her that if she would like to stop paying premiums, she can surrender part of those paid-up additions (PUAs) and use the CSV along with any additional dividends to cover the entire premium. While there are no guarantees, it looks as though Grace can do this indefinitely.
What is this process referred to as?
options: A. Reduced paid-up insurance
B. Paid-up additions (PUAs) dividend option
C. Premium offset
D. Limited payment whole life insurance
optionFeedback: Reduced paid-up insurance is a non-forfeiture option that permanently reduces the face amount; it is not a premium-offset strategy.
The PUA dividend option is how the dividends were accumulated — it is not the same as using accumulated PUAs to cover future premiums.
Limited payment whole life has a set number of payment years decided at purchase, not a strategy using accumulated PUAs.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Reduced paid-up insurance is a non-forfeiture option that permanently reduces the face amount; it is not a premium-offset strategy; The PUA dividend option is how the dividends were accumulated — it is not the same as using accumulated PUAs to cover future premiums.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Premium offset.

questions.question: Kimberly purchased a T-100 policy a few months ago. She is confused about future premiums associated with the policy. What should you tell her about most T-100 policies?
options: A. They apply a level premium approach throughout the life of the policy.
B. The premium will increase each policy year.
C. The premium is guaranteed to remain level for a certain number of years, after which the premium can be adjusted upwards but not downwards.
D. The premium will remain level for a certain number of years and then will be increased or decreased depending on what transpires with mortality costs, investment returns, and mortality rates.
optionFeedback: T-100 is not a yearly renewable term policy; the premium does not increase annually.
This describes an adjustable T-100 variant, not most standard T-100 policies.
While adjustable versions exist, most T-100 policies carry a fully level and guaranteed premium.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. T-100 is not a yearly renewable term policy; the premium does not increase annually; This describes an adjustable T-100 variant, not most standard T-100 policies.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: They apply a level premium approach throughout the life of the policy.

questions.question: Whole life insurance is more expensive than term insurance. Which of the following things are guaranteed on a whole life policy?
options: A. The death benefit.
B. The cash value.
C. The premium.
D. All of the above.
optionFeedback: The death benefit is guaranteed, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
The cash value is also guaranteed on a whole life policy, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
The premium is also guaranteed on a whole life policy, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The death benefit is guaranteed, but it is not the only guaranteed feature; The cash value is also guaranteed on a whole life policy, but it is not the only guaranteed feature.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: All of the above.

questions.question:
Carlos is looking to purchase a permanent insurance policy and would like the plan with the lowest annual premium.
Which policy would best suit his needs?
options: A. Non-participating whole life policy
B. Universal life
C. Participating whole life policy
D. Term-100
optionFeedback: Non-participating whole life is less expensive than participating WL, but still more expensive than T-100.
Universal life premiums depend on the deposit chosen, but for basic permanent coverage, T-100 is typically cheaper.
Participating whole life is the most expensive due to built-in conservative pricing for dividend potential.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Non-participating whole life is less expensive than participating WL, but still more expensive than T-100; Universal life premiums depend on the deposit chosen, but for basic permanent coverage, T-100 is typically cheaper.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Term-100.

questions.question:
Olivia purchased a participating policy last year. She has yet to receive a policy dividend. She is unsure of what circumstances would likely result in a dividend being paid.
Which of the following would most likely contribute to a policy dividend?
options: A. Lower than expected mortality rates.
B. Higher than expected investment returns.
C. Lower than expected administrative expenses.
D. All of the above.
optionFeedback: Lower mortality than expected can contribute to a surplus.
Higher investment returns can contribute to a surplus.
Lower expenses can contribute to a surplus.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Lower mortality than expected can contribute to a surplus; Higher investment returns can contribute to a surplus.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: All of the above.

questions.question:
Curtis has a $500,000 whole life policy with over $90,000 in cash value. His daughter, Chloe, is the named beneficiary. The monthly premium is $600. Curtis was travelling through Africa for a period of six months. During that time, his bank account went into overdraft, causing $3,600 in premium payments to be returned non-sufficient funds to the insurer. Tragically, Curtis was killed when he was trampled by a herd of elephants.
How much can his daughter expect to receive from the life insurance policy?
options: A. The policy would have lapsed after the 30-day grace period for missed premiums. A cheque would have been sent to Curtis for the cash surrender value. This payout will form part of the estate and will be distributed in accordance with his will.
B. $500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.
C. Nothing; the policy would have lapsed after a grace period (usually 30 days).
D. $500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.
optionFeedback: With $90,000+ in CSV, the APL non-forfeiture option would have automatically taken over — the policy would not have lapsed, and no CSV cheque would have been sent.
The policy would not have lapsed because the APL feature automatically uses the CSV to pay overdue premiums when the CSV is sufficient.

"$500,000 less any balance owing under an APL." is a different beneficiary designation than the one this question is testing. The better answer here is "$500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.".
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. With $90,000+ in CSV, the APL non-forfeiture option would have automatically taken over — the policy would not have lapsed, and no CSV cheque would have been sent; The policy would not have lapsed because the APL feature automatically uses the CSV to pay overdue premiums when the CSV is sufficient.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: $500,000 less any balance owing under an APL.

questions.question: After discussing the various dividend options available on a participating policy, Gabriel is leaning towards the PUA dividend option. Why is it a good idea for the agent to show her more than one dividend projection?
options: A. To illustrate the impact of different dividend scales.
B. To comply with provincial regulations.
C. To show the flexibility of the policy.
D. To demonstrate the impact of policy loans.
optionFeedback: The purpose is not simply to satisfy a provincial regulation requirement.
The core reason is to compare how different dividend scales affect outcomes, not merely to show general flexibility.
Policy loans are separate from dividend scale illustrations.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The purpose is not simply to satisfy a provincial regulation requirement; The core reason is to compare how different dividend scales affect outcomes, not merely to show general flexibility.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: To illustrate the impact of different dividend scales.

questions.question:
Julius owns a cottage. Upon his death, he would like to bequeath the cottage to his children. Julius is inquiring about life insurance policies that would provide the required funds to address the tax liability on the cottage. While he estimates that the tax liability will be approximately $300,000, he acknowledges that the property has already appreciated more than what he had originally estimated.
Julius also has a $200,000 mortgage with a 15-year amortization registered against the cottage and he would also like that to be paid in full upon his death. Julius has come to you for advice and would like to address his insurance needs in the most cost efficient manner.
Which one of the following policies would you recommend?
options: A. $300,000 term-to-100 policy and a $200,000 20-year term insurance rider.
B. $500,000 Term-100 policy.
C. $300,000 universal life insurance policy with level death benefit and a 10-year decreasing term insurance policy.
D. $300,000 whole life participating policy with a PUA dividend option, and a $200,000 Term 10 removable rider.
optionFeedback: T-100 does not grow, so it cannot keep pace with the increasing capital gains tax liability as the cottage continues to appreciate.
A single $500,000 T-100 overfunds the permanent need (capital gains) and inefficiently insures the temporary mortgage need as permanent coverage.
Universal life is more expensive than whole life participating for this purpose, and the 10-year decreasing term does not cover the full 15-year mortgage amortization.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. T-100 does not grow, so it cannot keep pace with the increasing capital gains tax liability as the cottage continues to appreciate; A single $500,000 T-100 overfunds the permanent need (capital gains) and inefficiently insures the temporary mortgage need as permanent coverage.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: $300,000 whole life participating policy with a PUA dividend option, and a $200,000 Term 10 removable rider.

questions.question: Amanda purchased a $1,000,000 participating whole life policy 14 months ago at the age of 30. The policy performed very well, resulting in a modest policy dividend after the first year. Amanda recently passed away. Which dividend option would have most likely resulted in the largest death benefit?
options: A. Investment in a segregated fund
B. One-year term addition dividend option
C. Accumulation dividend option
D. Paid-up addition (PUA) dividend option
optionFeedback: Investing the dividend in a segregated fund is not a standard participating policy dividend option.
Accumulation leaves the dividend on deposit with the insurer earning interest — it does not purchase additional life insurance coverage.
PUAs purchase paid-up whole life insurance, which provides less face amount per dividend dollar than one-year term.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Investing the dividend in a segregated fund is not a standard participating policy dividend option; Accumulation leaves the dividend on deposit with the insurer earning interest — it does not purchase additional life insurance coverage.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: One-year term addition dividend option.

questions.question:
Larry suffered a severe back injury that required surgery. He had to take a leave of absence from work in order to properly recover. He is likely to earn very little income while he is recovering. As a result, he does not want to concern himself with paying insurance premiums. He wants a solution that can be "undone" (reversed) when he is back to work earning his full income.
Which of the non-forfeiture options would you recommend?
options: A. Surrender the policy and receive the CSV
B. APL
C. Extended term insurance
D. Reduced paid-up insurance
optionFeedback: Surrendering the policy permanently terminates coverage — it cannot be undone.
Extended term insurance converts the policy to a paid-up term plan — while it can sometimes be reinstated, it is not easily reversible.
Reduced paid-up insurance permanently reduces the face amount — it is not easily reversible.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Surrendering the policy permanently terminates coverage — it cannot be undone; Extended term insurance converts the policy to a paid-up term plan — while it can sometimes be reinstated, it is not easily reversible.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: APL.

questions.question:
Michael owns a whole life policy. When he bought the policy, he was told about the non-forfeiture options.
Which of the following is not a non-forfeiture option?
options: A. Reduced paid-up insurance
B. Extended term insurance
C. Cash surrender value
D. Policy loan
optionFeedback: Reduced paid-up insurance is a standard non-forfeiture option.
Extended term insurance is a standard non-forfeiture option.
Cash surrender value is a standard non-forfeiture option.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Reduced paid-up insurance is a standard non-forfeiture option; Extended term insurance is a standard non-forfeiture option.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Policy loan.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is false about whole life insurance?
options: A. Whole life can be renewable and convertible.
B. Whole life offers non-forfeiture options.
C. Once whole life is issued, coverage is available for life regardless of age.
D. Whole life can be participating or non-participating.
optionFeedback: Whole life policies do offer non-forfeiture options (APL, extended term, reduced paid-up, and surrender for CSV).
Whole life coverage continues for the life of the insured (to age 100 when it endows).
Whole life can indeed be structured as either participating or non-participating.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Whole life policies do offer non-forfeiture options (APL, extended term, reduced paid-up, and surrender for CSV); Whole life coverage continues for the life of the insured (to age 100 when it endows).
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Whole life can be renewable and convertible.

questions.question: Amanda is purchasing a whole life policy. Her agent has discussed the various premium payment options. Assuming she lives until age 100, which of the following would result in the lowest total premiums paid?
options: A. Single premium
B. 10-pay life
C. 20-pay life
D. Life-pay (continuous premiums)
optionFeedback: 20-pay whole life requires 20 years of higher annual premiums, which totals more than a single premium in nominal terms.
10-pay whole life compresses payments into 10 years at an even higher annual amount, totalling more than a single premium.
Paying annually until age 100 results in many decades of payments that in nominal total exceed the single lump-sum premium.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. 20-pay whole life requires 20 years of higher annual premiums, which totals more than a single premium in nominal terms; 10-pay whole life compresses payments into 10 years at an even higher annual amount, totalling more than a single premium.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Single premium.

questions.question:
Caleb and Mona have a 25-year-old son who is severely ill and needs constant care. His life expectancy is another 20 years. He currently requires long-term care, which costs them approximately $20,000 per year. They are able to cover the costs from their incomes but would not be able to do so in the event of one of their deaths.
What would you recommend to address their concern?
options: A. Purchase a critical illness policy with Robert as the insured.
B. Purchase life insurance on Robert's life.
C. Purchase joint first-to-die with Caleb and Mona as the lives-insured.
D. Purchase joint last-to-die with Caleb and Mona as the lives-insured.
optionFeedback: A critical illness policy on Robert addresses Robert's health events, not the loss of parental income to fund his care.
Insuring Robert's life would pay upon Robert's death — it does not address the income gap created by a parent's death.
Joint last-to-die pays only after both parents have died — but the concern is that the care cannot be funded after the FIRST death.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A critical illness policy on Robert addresses Robert's health events, not the loss of parental income to fund his care; Insuring Robert's life would pay upon Robert's death — it does not address the income gap created by a parent's death.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Purchase joint first-to-die with Caleb and Mona as the lives-insured.

questions.question: Assuming an insurance company offers such a policy (which would probably not be the case), which of the following amounts do you think would be closest to the annual premium for a 99-year-old purchasing $100,000 of one-year term insurance?
optionFeedback: $1,000 is far too low for a 99-year-old because the probability of death is extremely high.
$5,000 still significantly underestimates the near-certain probability of death at age 99.
$10,000 remains far too low given the near-certainty of death within one year at age 99.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. $1,000 is far too low for a 99-year-old because the probability of death is extremely high; $5,000 still significantly underestimates the near-certain probability of death at age 99.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: $90,000.

questions.question: Harold is explaining to his client how the premium on a yearly renewable term insurance policy would compare to the premium on a permanent insurance plan. All else being equal, which explanation is most accurate?
options: A. The premium on a yearly renewable term policy will increase yearly until it plateaus at a level that is similar to the permanent plan premium. The downside is that the yearly renewable term policy will be renewable only until a certain maximum age.

B. The premium on a yearly renewable term insurance policy would be lower than the premium on a permanent plan in the first year. The premium on the yearly renewable term policy would increase at each renewal period and would eventually be more than the premium on the permanent plan.
C. The premiums would be very similar in all policy years; however, the term insurance will be renewable only until a certain maximum age.
D. Permanent insurance is more expensive than yearly renewable term insurance, and therefore the premium is higher in all policy years.
optionFeedback: The YRT premium does not plateau at the level of the permanent plan premium — it continues to increase and will eventually exceed it.
The premiums are not similar in all years; YRT starts much lower and increases each year.
Permanent insurance is more expensive in the early years, but the YRT premium will eventually surpass it — permanent insurance is NOT more expensive in all policy years.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The YRT premium does not plateau at the level of the permanent plan premium — it continues to increase and will eventually exceed it; The premiums are not similar in all years; YRT starts much lower and increases each year.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: The premium on a yearly renewable term insurance policy would be lower than the premium on a permanent plan in the first year. The premium on the yearly renewable term policy would increase at each renewal period and would eventually be more than the premium on the permanent plan.

questions.question:
Adam owns a cottage that he would like to pass along to his only child upon his death. The cottage was originally purchased for $200,000 and is currently worth over $1 million. Upon Adam's death, capital gains taxes will be triggered. Adam would like a life insurance policy that would pay out a death benefit equal to the anticipated capital gains tax.
Adam earns a very good income and therefore is not concerned about ever missing a premium or letting the policy lapse. He does, however, loathe the thought of spending money on insurance. He therefore wants the lowest-cost solution that will also meet his needs.
Which of the following policies would you recommend?
options: A. Whole life insurance
B. Renewable 10-year term insurance
C. Term-to-100
D. Universal life insurance
optionFeedback: Whole life insurance is permanent but more expensive than T-100 due to its cash value component.
Renewable term insurance is not permanent — the coverage may lapse or become too expensive later in life, and the capital gains tax liability is a permanent need.
Universal life is permanent with investment flexibility but carries higher premiums than T-100 for basic coverage.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Whole life insurance is permanent but more expensive than T-100 due to its cash value component; Renewable term insurance is not permanent — the coverage may lapse or become too expensive later in life, and the capital gains tax liability is a permanent need.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Term-to-100.

questions.question:
Darcy owns three different life insurance policies. He understands that when the insurer sets the premium on a policy, they consider estimated investment returns, expenses of running the policy, and of course, mortality rates. Darcy mentions to you that he continually hears about the Bank of Canada lowering interest rates. He is concerned that the decline in interest rates will result in the insurer increasing the premium on one or more of his policies.
Which policy could be impacted by the change in interest rates?
options: A. Guaranteed whole life insurance
B. Non-participating whole life insurance
C. Whole life insurance
D. Adjustable whole life
optionFeedback: A guaranteed whole life policy has fixed, unchangeable premiums regardless of economic conditions.
Non-participating whole life also has guaranteed, fixed premiums that the insurer cannot adjust.
Traditional whole life (guaranteed) premiums are locked in at issue and are not subject to interest rate changes.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A guaranteed whole life policy has fixed, unchangeable premiums regardless of economic conditions; Non-participating whole life also has guaranteed, fixed premiums that the insurer cannot adjust.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Adjustable whole life.

questions.question:
Jules and Sandra have been married for 20 years. They own a cottage that was purchased for only $100,000. Today the cottage is estimated to be worth $900,000. The cottage is not their principal residence, so it would eventually attract capital gains taxes. They would like to gift the property to their only son upon their deaths and do not want the value of their estate to be eroded by the capital gains tax. As a result, they are purchasing participating whole life insurance.
Sandra is ecstatic every time she learns the cottage has appreciated in value, but then complains that it will only result in more capital gains tax.
What dividend option should they select specifically to address Sandra's concern?
options: A. Term insurance
B. Premium offset
C. Paid-up additions (PUAs)
D. Accumulation
optionFeedback: The term insurance dividend option adds only temporary coverage — it would not grow the permanent death benefit to keep pace with increasing capital gains taxes.
Premium offset uses dividends to pay the base premium — it does not increase the death benefit to address a growing tax liability.
Accumulation leaves dividends on deposit earning interest, but does not add permanent insurance coverage to match the growing capital gains tax liability.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The term insurance dividend option adds only temporary coverage — it would not grow the permanent death benefit to keep pace with increasing capital gains taxes; Premium offset uses dividends to pay the base premium — it does not increase the death benefit to address a growing tax liability.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Paid-up additions (PUAs).

questions.question:
Your client purchased a whole life policy 30 years ago. He is now in his early 90's and wonders for how much longer he is required to pay the premium.
What should you tell him?
options: A. He is required to pay the premium until he dies, until surrenders the policy, or until he reaches age 100. If he lives to be age 100, then the policy would automatically end and he would no longer have coverage.
B. He is required to pay the premium until he surrenders the policy or until he dies.
C. Whole life policies typically endow at age 90. Now that he is 90, he is not required to make any more premium payments and he would still retain his coverage.
D. He is required to pay the premium until he dies, until the policy is surrendered, or until he reaches age 100, whichever comes first.
optionFeedback: This is mostly correct but misleading — at age 100, the policy does not simply 'end with no coverage.' It endows, meaning the insured receives the face value as an endowment payment.
This omits the important age-100 endowment provision — premiums stop and the policy matures at age 100.
Whole life policies endow at age 100, not age 90.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. This is mostly correct but misleading — at age 100, the policy does not simply 'end with no coverage.' It endows, meaning the insured receives the face value as an endowment payment; This omits the important age-100 endowment provision — premiums stop and the policy matures at age 100.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: He is required to pay the premium until he dies, until the policy is surrendered, or until he reaches age 100, whichever comes first.

questions.question:
All else being equal, which of the following policies would be the most expensive?
Note: A waiver of premium rider waives the premium if the policyholder is disabled. This feature is discussed in a different chapter.
options: A. Participating whole life insurance with a waiver of premium
B. Non-participating whole life insurance
C. Participating whole life insurance
D. Non-participating whole life insurance with a convertible feature
optionFeedback: Non-participating WL is less expensive than participating WL and has no additional rider.

"Participating whole life insurance" is a different statement than the one this question is testing. The correct answer is "Participating whole life insurance with a waiver of premium" because this feature keeps the policy in force when the insured qualifies for a disability waiver.
A convertible feature adds a small cost, but non-participating WL + convertible is still less expensive than participating WL + waiver of premium.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Non-participating WL is less expensive than participating WL and has no additional rider; Participating WL is more expensive than non-participating WL, but adding a waiver of premium rider makes option A even more expensive.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Participating whole life insurance with a waiver of premium.

questions.question: All else being equal, which of the following policies would result in the highest annual premium?
options: A. Non-participating whole life
B. Participating whole life insurance
D. Limited payment participating whole life insurance
optionFeedback: Non-participating WL is less expensive than participating WL annually.
Participating WL has a higher annual premium than non-participating WL and T-100, but limited-pay compresses all premiums into fewer years.
T-100 is the lowest-cost permanent insurance option with no cash value.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Non-participating WL is less expensive than participating WL annually; Participating WL has a higher annual premium than non-participating WL and T-100, but limited-pay compresses all premiums into fewer years.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: Limited payment participating whole life insurance.

questions.question:
Harvey recently learned that he is terminally ill and most likely will die within the next two years. The prognosis has been confirmed by several doctors. Harvey has a $500,000 face value whole life policy that has a cash value of $200,000. The annual premium on the policy is $3,200.
Which of the following is an option available to Harvey?
options: A. He can continue to pay the premiums, keep the policy in force, and upon his death, the beneficiary will receive exactly $500,000.
B. He can continue to pay the premiums, keep the policy in force, and upon his death, the beneficiary will receive exactly $700,000.

C. He can stop making premiums and allow the APL non-forfeiture option to take care of the premium. Upon his death, his beneficiaries will receive the face amount of coverage plus the cash value of approximately $700,000.
D. He can stop paying premiums and allow the APL non-forfeiture option to take care of the premium. Upon his death, his beneficiaries will receive the face amount of coverage of $500,000.
optionFeedback: The death benefit on a whole life policy is the face value ($500,000) — not the face value plus the cash value. Cash value is not paid in addition to the death benefit.
The death benefit is the face value only, not face value plus cash value. Adding them to get $700,000 is incorrect.
While using APL is an option, the net death benefit would be the face value LESS the accumulated APL loan — not exactly $500,000.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The death benefit on a whole life policy is the face value ($500,000) — not the face value plus the cash value. Cash value is not paid in addition to the death benefit; The death benefit is the face value only, not face value plus cash value. Adding them to get $700,000 is incorrect.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: He can continue to pay the premiums, keep the policy in force, and upon his death, the beneficiary will receive exactly $500,000.

questions.question:
Bobby and Chrissy have a $500,000 whole life policy that currently has a $10,000 cash value. The annual premium on the policy is $3,600. They have no outstanding policy loans or overdue premiums. Bobby left Chrissy a "Honey, please do" list while he was away on a two-day business trip. He asked her to:

Call the insurer and find out how much they would receive if they surrendered their life insurance policy.

Call the dealership and find out how much it would cost to buy out their car lease.

Get a quote on a planned expansion of their home.

Call the real estate agent to find out how much the cottage they viewed is listed for.

Chrissy completed the entire list and wrote the four numbers down on the same piece of paper. She is now having trouble remembering which number pertains to which. Of the four numbers, which is most likely to be the amount they would receive if surrendering the policy?
optionFeedback: $10,500 exceeds the stated cash value of $10,000; the surrender value is typically slightly less than, not more than, the cash value due to surrender charges.
$498,000 and $508,000 are far too large to be a life insurance surrender value — these are more consistent with a cottage listing price or home renovation quote.
$508,000 is far too large to be a life insurance surrender value.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. $10,500 exceeds the stated cash value of $10,000; the surrender value is typically slightly less than, not more than, the cash value due to surrender charges; $498,000 and $508,000 are far too large to be a life insurance surrender value — these are more consistent with a cottage listing price or home renovation quote.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: $9,700.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes adjustable whole life insurance?
options: A. The premium and coverage are guaranteed only for a certain number of years. After the guaranteed period, the insurer reserves the right to decrease the premium but not increase it, based on actual experience.
B. The premium and coverage are guaranteed only for a certain number of years. After the guaranteed period, the insurer can increase the coverage without evidence of insurability.
C. The premium and coverage are guaranteed only for a certain number of years. After the guaranteed period, the insurer reserves the right to adjust the premium.

D. The premium and coverage are guaranteed only for a certain number of years. After the guaranteed period, the insurer reserves the right to increase the premium but not decrease it, based on actual experience.
optionFeedback: The insurer can adjust the premium in either direction (up or down), not only decrease it.
Adjustable whole life refers to premium adjustments by the insurer, not the insurer unilaterally increasing coverage without evidence.
The insurer can adjust the premium both upward and downward based on actual experience — not only upward.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. The insurer can adjust the premium in either direction (up or down), not only decrease it; Adjustable whole life refers to premium adjustments by the insurer, not the insurer unilaterally increasing coverage without evidence.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The premium and coverage are guaranteed only for a certain number of years. After the guaranteed period, the insurer reserves the right to adjust the premium.

questions.question:
Alex purchased a participating whole life policy and selected the PUA dividend option. The policy was purchased to cover capital gains tax on his cottage upon his death. Alex is counting on the increased coverage to offset the fact that the value of the cottage, and in turn, the tax liability, is likely to increase. Alex's agent advised him that participating policies are more expensive than non-participating policies.
Why is this?
options: A. They offer higher death benefits.
B. The insurer charges a higher premium to cover the possibility of poor performance and potential dividends.
C. They have higher administrative costs.
D. They include more riders.
optionFeedback: Participating policies do not inherently offer higher death benefits than non-participating policies.
Higher administrative costs are not the main reason for the higher premium.
Riders are optional and not the reason participating policies cost more.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Participating policies do not inherently offer higher death benefits than non-participating policies; Higher administrative costs are not the main reason for the higher premium.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: The insurer charges a higher premium to cover the possibility of poor performance and potential dividends.

questions.question:
Michael owns a whole life policy that has a face value of $800,000 and a cash value of approximately $100,000. The annual premium on the policy is $8,000, payable until age 100. Michael is considering taking a five-year sabbatical to tour the world and write a thesis.
While he is not concerned about the loss of income or increased expenses during that time, he asks if there is some way he can have relief from paying the premium on his policy. His only stipulation is that he would like to have the same level of coverage for a minimum of five years. He is adamant that if he dies within the next five years, his beneficiary must receive exactly $800,000.
Which of the following options would meet Michael's needs?
options: A. He could surrender his policy.
B. He could have the premiums paid by automatic premium loan (APL).
C. He could choose the extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.
D. He can exercise the reduced paid-up non-forfeiture option.
optionFeedback: Surrendering the policy permanently terminates coverage — the beneficiary would receive nothing upon death.
APL keeps the policy in force, but the death benefit would be $800,000 MINUS the accumulated APL loan balance — not exactly $800,000 as required.
Reduced paid-up insurance provides permanent coverage for a lesser face amount — not exactly $800,000 as required.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Surrendering the policy permanently terminates coverage — the beneficiary would receive nothing upon death; APL keeps the policy in force, but the death benefit would be $800,000 MINUS the accumulated APL loan balance — not exactly $800,000 as required.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: He could choose the extended term insurance non-forfeiture option.

questions.question:
A life insurance policy has an annual premium of $1,000. Given the modal factors below, which statement is correct regarding the required payments?
| Premium Frequency | Modal Factor |
| :--- | :--- |
| Annual | N/A |
| Semi-annual | 0.51 |
| Quarterly | 0.26 |
| Monthly | 0.09 |
options: A. If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay 9% more as compared to paying the premium on an annual basis.
B. If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay $80 more than the stated annual premium.
C. The semi-annual payment would result in the highest total annual premium.
D. The monthly premium would be equal to 1/12 of the annual premium.
optionFeedback: Monthly total = $90 × 12 = $1,080. That is 8% more than $1,000, not 9%.
Monthly total ($1,080) is higher than semi-annual total ($510 × 2 = $1,020) — monthly results in the highest total annual premium.
1/12 of $1,000 = $83.33, but the modal factor gives a monthly payment of $90 — these are not equal.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Monthly total = $90 × 12 = $1,080. That is 8% more than $1,000, not 9%; Monthly total ($1,080) is higher than semi-annual total ($510 × 2 = $1,020) — monthly results in the highest total annual premium.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: If the policyholder opts for monthly payments, he or she will pay $80 more than the stated annual premium.

questions.question:
Marcy owns a 30-year limited pay term 100 policy. She is unsure what the word "limited" refers to.
What should you tell her?
options: A. Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would be paid-up and she would retain the coverage.
B. Marcy will be required to pay premiums for a maximum of 30 years. After that, she must convert the policy into a permanent plan or else it will terminate.
C. Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would mature and she would have no more coverage.
D. The coverage will only be in place (limited) for 30 years.
optionFeedback: A limited pay T-100 does not need to be converted — it is already permanent (T-100). After the payment period, the policy is simply paid-up.
A paid-up T-100 does not mature at the end of the payment period — coverage continues for life (to age 100).
'Limited' refers to the premium payment period being limited to 30 years, not the duration of the coverage.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A limited pay T-100 does not need to be converted — it is already permanent (T-100). After the payment period, the policy is simply paid-up; A paid-up T-100 does not mature at the end of the payment period — coverage continues for life (to age 100).
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Marcy will only be required to pay premiums for 30 years, after which point the policy would be paid-up and she would retain the coverage.

questions.question:
Biff has an adjustable whole life policy. The policy offers a guaranteed premium for the first five years, but it can be adjusted by the insurer thereafter.
Which of the following variables could impact the adjustment?
options: A. Change in administrative costs
B. Change in interest rates
C. Change in mortality rates
D. All of the above
optionFeedback: Administrative costs are one factor but not the only one.
Interest rates are one factor but not the only one.
Mortality rates are one factor but not the only one.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Administrative costs are one factor but not the only one; Interest rates are one factor but not the only one.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: All of the above.

questions.question: Archie purchased a whole life participating policy and selected the PUA dividend option. Which of the following statements is true regarding this PUA?
options: A. The PUA will not likely have a cash value.
B. The PUA will carry a term insurance addition option.
C. The PUA will require additional premiums.
D. The PUA is permanent insurance.
optionFeedback: Paid-up additions are whole life insurance and do accumulate their own cash value.
PUAs are paid-up whole life additions — they do not carry a separate term insurance option.
PUAs are 'paid-up' — no additional premiums are ever required for them.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Paid-up additions are whole life insurance and do accumulate their own cash value; PUAs are paid-up whole life additions — they do not carry a separate term insurance option.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option D: The PUA is permanent insurance.

questions.question: Gracie purchased a participating whole life insurance policy and selected the PUA dividend option. She selected this option because it will result in increased coverage. What type of insurance is added?
options: A. Term rider
B. One-year term insurance
C. Whole life insurance
optionFeedback: PUAs are not term riders — they are paid-up whole life additions that do not expire.
One-year term insurance is a separate dividend option — not the PUA option.
PUAs are whole life insurance, not T-100. T-100 is a different permanent product.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. PUAs are not term riders — they are paid-up whole life additions that do not expire; One-year term insurance is a separate dividend option — not the PUA option.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Whole life insurance.

questions.question:
Larry suffered a severe back injury that required surgery. He had to take a leave of absence from work in order to properly recover after the surgery. He is likely to earn very little income while he is recovering.
Which of the following non-forfeiture options would best suit his needs?
options: A. Surrender the policy
B. APL
C. Extended term insurance
D. Reduced paid-up insurance
optionFeedback: Surrendering the policy permanently ends coverage — not appropriate for a temporary financial hardship.
Extended term insurance converts the policy to a paid-up term plan; while it suspends premium payments, it is harder to reverse if Larry wants to resume original coverage later.
Reduced paid-up insurance permanently reduces the face amount — not ideal for a temporary situation.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Surrendering the policy permanently ends coverage — not appropriate for a temporary financial hardship; Extended term insurance converts the policy to a paid-up term plan; while it suspends premium payments, it is harder to reverse if Larry wants to resume original coverage later.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: APL.

questions.question:
Snap, Crackle, Pop, and Toucan are four friends who each bought different permanent insurance plans. They recently embarked on a once-in-a-lifetime 60-day trek to Africa, where they have no contact with anyone back home. Nearing the end of their trip, they all realize they forgot to make payments to their respective insurance policies.
All else being equal, which of the following policies is most likely to have lapsed?
options: B. Limited payment T-100
C. Participating whole life policy
D. Non-participating whole life policy
optionFeedback: A limited payment T-100 that has completed its payment period requires no further premiums and cannot lapse due to missed payments.
A participating whole life policy accumulates cash value, which can fund the APL non-forfeiture option to prevent lapsing.
A non-participating whole life policy also accumulates cash value, which can fund the APL to prevent lapsing.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A limited payment T-100 that has completed its payment period requires no further premiums and cannot lapse due to missed payments; A participating whole life policy accumulates cash value, which can fund the APL non-forfeiture option to prevent lapsing.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: T-100.

questions.question:
Becky is an animal lover who spends much of her spare time volunteering at animal sanctuaries and is an activist for animal rights. She practices what she preaches and has adopted a vegan diet. She would like to purchase a permanent life insurance policy that will allow her to gift money to various charities upon her death.
Becky would like to pay the lowest possible premium, but there is one caveat; she acknowledges that she is very young and her life goals may change. As a result, there is a possibility that she may surrender the policy at some point down the road. She would also like to keep the premiums as low as possible.
Which of the following policies best meets her needs?
options: A. Whole life insurance
B. 10-year term insurance
C. Universal life insurance
D. Term-100
optionFeedback: Whole life insurance provides a CSV upon surrender but carries a higher premium than universal life for the same basic coverage.
10-year term insurance is not permanent — it would not allow her to gift money to charities upon death at an unknown future date.
T-100 has the lowest premium among permanent policies, but it typically has no cash surrender value — if Becky surrenders it, she receives nothing.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Whole life insurance provides a CSV upon surrender but carries a higher premium than universal life for the same basic coverage; 10-year term insurance is not permanent — it would not allow her to gift money to charities upon death at an unknown future date.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Universal life insurance.

questions.question:
Chloe purchased a participating whole life policy because she requires permanent insurance. She would like to select a dividend option that will add the most coverage in the early years.
Which dividend option is suitable given Chloe's goals?
options: A. Paid-up additions (PUAs)
B. Term insurance
C. Accumulation
D. Premium reduction
optionFeedback: PUAs purchase permanent whole life insurance with each dividend, but at a lower face amount per dollar than one-year term insurance.
Accumulation leaves dividends on deposit earning interest — it does not add life insurance coverage.
Premium reduction reduces the out-of-pocket premium but does not increase coverage.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. PUAs purchase permanent whole life insurance with each dividend, but at a lower face amount per dollar than one-year term insurance; Accumulation leaves dividends on deposit earning interest — it does not add life insurance coverage.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: Term insurance.

questions.question: Samuel purchased a non-participating adjustable whole life policy. How would an increase in interest rates affect his policy?
options: A. It would decrease the expected policy dividend.
B. It would increase his premium.
C. It would decrease his premium.
D. It would increase the expected policy dividend.
optionFeedback: A non-participating policy pays no dividends — dividend effects are irrelevant.
Higher interest rates mean the insurer earns more on reserves, reducing the required premium — not increasing it.
A non-participating policy pays no dividends regardless of investment performance.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. A non-participating policy pays no dividends — dividend effects are irrelevant; Higher interest rates mean the insurer earns more on reserves, reducing the required premium — not increasing it.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: It would decrease his premium.

questions.question: Derrick purchased a $200,000 participating whole life policy five years ago. He is unsure of the dividend option he selected. He does recall that his agent stressed the dividend would be used to purchase permanent insurance, which would result in his coverage increasing each time a dividend is paid. What dividend option does this describe?
options: A. PUA
B. Premium reduction
C. One-year term insurance dividend option
D. Accumulation dividend option
optionFeedback: Premium reduction uses the dividend to offset the next premium payment — it does not purchase additional insurance coverage.
The one-year term insurance dividend option purchases temporary one-year term coverage — not permanent insurance.
Accumulation leaves dividends on deposit with the insurer earning interest — it does not purchase additional insurance.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Premium reduction uses the dividend to offset the next premium payment — it does not purchase additional insurance coverage; The one-year term insurance dividend option purchases temporary one-year term coverage — not permanent insurance.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: PUA.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a dividend option available on a participating whole life policy?
options: A. One-year term insurance
B. Paid-up additions (PUAs)
C. Reduced paid-up insurance
D. Premium reduction
optionFeedback: One-year term insurance is a standard dividend option — the dividend is used to purchase a temporary term rider.
PUAs are a standard dividend option — the dividend is used to purchase paid-up whole life additions.
Premium reduction is a standard dividend option — the dividend reduces the next scheduled premium payment.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. One-year term insurance is a standard dividend option — the dividend is used to purchase a temporary term rider; PUAs are a standard dividend option — the dividend is used to purchase paid-up whole life additions.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Reduced paid-up insurance.

questions.question:
Ozzieia is purchasing a whole life participating policy. She has a 13-year-old daughter and would like to have as much coverage as possible for the next few years.
Which dividend option would be most suitable for her?
options: A. One-year term insurance
B. PUA
C. Accumulation
D. Cash payment
optionFeedback: PUA adds permanent coverage, but not as much near-term coverage per dividend dollar as one-year term insurance.
Accumulation leaves dividends on deposit and does not maximize coverage over the next few years.
Cash payment does not add insurance coverage.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. PUA adds permanent coverage, but not as much near-term coverage per dividend dollar as one-year term insurance; Accumulation leaves dividends on deposit and does not maximize coverage over the next few years.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: One-year term insurance.

questions.question:
Michael is a middle-aged man. The annual premium on his whole life policy is approximately $9,000. He bought the policy shortly after graduating from university. The policy now has a cash value of approximately $100,000. He is considering taking a five-year sabbatical to tour the world and write a thesis.
His insurance needs have changed. He does not need as much coverage today and he no longer wants to pay a premium, but he would still like to have permanent coverage even if it is for a lesser amount.
Which of the following would be a potential solution?
options: A. Reduced paid-up insurance non-forfeiture option
B. Reduced paid-up insurance dividend option
C. Extended term insurance dividend option
D. Extended term insurance non-forfeiture option
optionFeedback: There is no 'reduced paid-up insurance dividend option' — reduced paid-up insurance is a non-forfeiture option, not a dividend option.
There is no 'extended term insurance dividend option' — extended term insurance is a non-forfeiture option, not a dividend option.
Extended term insurance is a non-forfeiture option that preserves the original face amount but for a limited term — Michael wants permanent coverage, not temporary term coverage.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. There is no 'reduced paid-up insurance dividend option' — reduced paid-up insurance is a non-forfeiture option, not a dividend option; There is no 'extended term insurance dividend option' — extended term insurance is a non-forfeiture option, not a dividend option.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: Reduced paid-up insurance non-forfeiture option.

questions.question:
Meagan is interested in purchasing a $100,000 face value guaranteed whole life policy. The premium on the policy will be $150 per month. Meagan understands that when setting premiums on a whole life policy, the insurer must make certain assumptions.
Which of the following statements is most correct regarding those assumptions?
options: A. If the insurer's experience is better than assumed, they will reduce the premium.
B. If the insurer's experience is worse than assumed, they will increase the premium.
C. The insurer cannot change the premium even if their actual experience is different from their assumptions.
D. The insurer can only change the premium with the policyholder's consent.
optionFeedback: This is a guaranteed whole life policy — the premium is fixed for life and is not reduced if experience is better than assumed.
A guaranteed whole life policy does not allow the insurer to increase the premium after issue.
The insurer cannot change the premium at all, so policyholder consent does not create such a right.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. This is a guaranteed whole life policy — the premium is fixed for life and is not reduced if experience is better than assumed; A guaranteed whole life policy does not allow the insurer to increase the premium after issue.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The insurer cannot change the premium even if their actual experience is different from their assumptions.

questions.question: Betsy is weighing the pros and cons of purchasing a participating versus non-participating whole life policy. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: A. On a participating policy, if the policy realizes a shortfall, the insurer will charge an additional premium assessment that the policyholder would have to pay.
B. Participating policies allow the life-insured to spread death benefits among multiple beneficiaries.
C. On a participating policy, if the policy realizes a surplus, the insurer will share the surplus with policy owners in the form of policy dividends.
D. A participating policy will have a lower premium as compared to a non-participating policy.
optionFeedback: On a participating policy, if there is a shortfall, the insurer absorbs the loss — the policyholder is never charged an additional assessment.
The ability to name multiple beneficiaries has nothing to do with whether the policy is participating or non-participating.
Participating policies have a HIGHER premium than non-participating policies, not lower — the higher premium reflects conservative pricing for potential dividend distribution.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. On a participating policy, if there is a shortfall, the insurer absorbs the loss — the policyholder is never charged an additional assessment; The ability to name multiple beneficiaries has nothing to do with whether the policy is participating or non-participating.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: On a participating policy, if the policy realizes a surplus, the insurer will share the surplus with policy owners in the form of policy dividends.

questions.question:
Jack, age 55, has a huge bank account but very limited annual cash flow. He is purchasing a sizeable whole life insurance policy as part of his estate plan. After doing a thorough insurance needs analysis, it has been determined that permanent coverage is appropriate. Jack would like to make just one premium payment each year until death or until age 80, whichever comes first.
Which premium option would be ideal for Jack?
options: A. Single premium
B. Adjustable whole life
C. Limited payment
D. Ongoing premium
optionFeedback: Single premium requires only one lump-sum payment upfront — Jack wants to make annual payments over a period of years, not one single payment.
Adjustable whole life refers to the insurer's ability to adjust the premium — it is a product type, not a payment option, and does not describe paying until age 80.
Ongoing premium (straight life) means paying annually until death or age 100 — Jack specifically wants to stop at age 80.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify what the question is asking (rule, calculation, or outcome).
Step 2: Apply the LLQP rule or formula that governs the scenario.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors using the facts. Single premium requires only one lump-sum payment upfront — Jack wants to make annual payments over a period of years, not one single payment; Adjustable whole life refers to the insurer's ability to adjust the premium — it is a product type, not a payment option, and does not describe paying until age 80.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: Limited payment.
